input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **Physical Twin system** being studied is a **complex engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing or industrial system) characterized by the following key attributes: **(1) Multi-layered abstraction** spanning requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers, as defined in **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT). **(2) Component-based structure** with explicit **component & connector perspectives**, modeled using **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. **(3) Behavioral complexity** requiring integration of **state machines (SysML v1.7), automata (MontiArc Automaton), or simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation engines) to model dynamic behavior. **(4) Heterogeneous stakeholder integration**, necessitating alignment across diverse modeling notations and tools. **(5) Operational domain** aligned with **manufacturing systems** (as inferred from the Digital Shadow reference model in Fig. 1), where physical objects generate **contextual data traces** for digital representation. The system’s suitability for digital twinning stems from its **highly interconnected, data-intensive, and behaviorally complex** nature, requiring **real-time or near-real-time data synchronization** between physical and digital layers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, categorized by functional capabilities and target stakeholders: **(1) Data Acquisition & Aggregation**: Passive collection of **contextual data traces** from the physical system (as defined in the **Digital Shadow Reference Model**), including **one-way automated data flow** from physical to digital layers. **(2) Data Interpretation & Processing**: Active components (e.g., **software agents or embedded logic**) interpret incoming data traces, enabling **abstraction, aggregation, and contextualization** for downstream services. **(3) Monitoring & Visualization**: Real-time or batch visualization of system states, leveraging **modeling tools (SysML, ADLs)** or **simulation platforms (Simulink)** to present **component interactions, interface states, and behavioral dynamics**. **(4) Predictive Analytics & Optimization**: Integration with **simulation engines** (e.g., Simulink, other tools) to perform **predictive modeling, scenario analysis, and optimization** of system performance, logistics, or resource allocation. **(5) Control & Autonomy**: While the **Digital Shadow** is passive, the **DT extends functionality** to include **active control loops** (e.g., via **state machines, automata, or code-based logic**) to influence physical system behavior, though explicit control mechanisms are not detailed in the document. **(6) Stakeholder-Specific Services**: Tailored outputs for **engineers (design/validation), operators (real-time monitoring), and managers (strategic optimization)**, requiring **heterogeneous model integration** across tools like **SysML, ADLs, and simulation platforms**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** relies on the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their roles in **modeling, simulation, data processing, and integration**: **(1) Modeling & Architecture Tools**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **multi-layered abstraction** (requirements, functional/logical architectures, product layers) and **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector modeling.
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **ACME, AADL, MontiArc** for **component-based system decomposition**, interface definition, and **composition rules**. MontiArc includes **automata-based behavior modeling**.
    - **Methodologies**: **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT** for structuring **complex system development** across abstraction layers.
  **(2) Simulation & Behavioral Modeling**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink**: For **detailed behavioral modeling**, dynamic system simulation, and **control logic implementation**.
    - **State Machines (SysML v1.7)**: To model **discrete event behavior** and transitions.
    - **MontiArc Automaton**: For **formal behavior specification** within ADL frameworks.
  **(3) Data & Integration Enablers**:
    - **Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1)**: Defines **UML-based class diagrams** for **contextual data trace aggregation** and **one-way data flow** from physical to digital layers.
    - **Heterogeneous Tool Integration**: Mechanisms to **bridge disparate modeling notations** (e.g., SysML ↔ Simulink ↔ ADLs) and **stakeholder perspectives**, though specific integration protocols are not detailed.
  **(4) General-Purpose Software**:
    - **C++/Java**: For **custom logic, control algorithms, or data processing** within the DT ecosystem.
  **(5) Database & Storage**: Not explicitly specified, but implied to support **contextual data trace storage** and **model persistence** across tools. The document does not mention **specific databases or storage technologies**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided document does not explicitly define multiplicities or hierarchical structures for Digital Twin (DT) instances. However, the context implies a **decentralized or modular architecture** is necessary due to the integration of heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives (as mentioned in Section II.A). The document references **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, and **SMArDT**, which inherently support hierarchical decomposition of systems into components, interfaces, and compositions. While no explicit coordination mechanisms are detailed, the use of **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** suggests a structured, potentially hierarchical approach to DT instances. The document also highlights the need for **active components** in DTs (contrasted with passive digital shadows), implying dynamic interactions that may require **centralized coordination** for synchronization or **decentralized autonomy** for localized decision-making. No specific multiplicities (e.g., 1:1, 1:N, or N:M mappings) or synchronization frequencies are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The **Digital Twin (DT) constellation** is implicitly defined through the **orchestration of heterogeneous modeling tools and methodologies** described in Section II.A. The document emphasizes a **multi-layered, multi-perspective approach** to system modeling, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**, which must be integrated into a cohesive DT system. Key orchestration mechanisms include:
  1. **Architecture Patterns**: The use of **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs** to define **component & connector perspectives**, including interfaces, interconnections, and composition. MontiArc, for example, extends this with **state machines** for behavioral modeling.
  2. **Tool Integration**: The DT constellation must integrate **diverse modeling notations and tools**, such as **SysML (for structural/logical modeling)**, **MathWorks Simulink (for behavioral/simulation modeling)**, and **general-purpose languages (C++, Java)** for implementation. This requires **interoperability frameworks** to bridge these tools, though no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST) are mentioned.
  3. **Resource Management**: The document implies **stakeholder-specific perspectives** must be harmonized, suggesting a **resource-aware orchestration** to balance computational load, data consistency, and real-time constraints across layers. The **digital shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) further suggests a **data-centric coordination** where aggregated contextual traces are processed by active DT components.
  4. **System-Wide Coordination**: While not explicitly detailed, the contrast between **digital shadows (passive, one-way data flow)** and **DTs (active, bidirectional interaction)** implies the constellation must support **event-driven or service-oriented coordination** to enable real-time adjustments between physical and digital domains. The lack of specific latency requirements or synchronization frequencies (e.g., millisecond-level updates) suggests this is domain-agnostic but dependent on the underlying MBSE methodology.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The document highlights **horizontal integration** as a critical challenge due to the **heterogeneous nature of Digital Twin (DT) systems**, requiring interoperability across **external systems, enterprise architectures, and other DTs**. Key aspects include:
  1. **Data Exchange Protocols**: While no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST, AMQP) are mentioned, the integration of **diverse modeling tools** (SysML, Simulink, C++, Java) implies the need for **standardized data serialization formats** (e.g., JSON, XML, Protocol Buffers) and **APIs** to facilitate cross-tool communication. The **digital shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) suggests a **contextual data aggregation layer**, which may rely on **event-driven or publish-subscribe mechanisms** for external data ingestion.
  2. **Interoperability Standards**: The document references **MBSE methodologies** (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) and **ADLs** (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), which inherently support **modularity and standardization** but do not specify industry-wide standards like **ISO 23247 (Digital Twin Framework)** or **IEC 62443 (Industrial Automation)**. Integration with **enterprise systems** (e.g., ERP, MES, PLM) would require **middleware solutions** to translate between domain-specific models and DT representations.
  3. **Dependencies on External Systems**: The DT’s ability to function depends on **real-time or near-real-time data feeds** from physical assets, sensors, or legacy systems. The document does not specify **latency tolerances** or **data freshness requirements**, but the **active processing** of digital shadows (vs. passive collection) implies **low-latency dependencies** for closed-loop control or predictive analytics. External systems may also include **third-party DTs or federated architectures**, necessitating **security and trust frameworks** (e.g., blockchain for data provenance, OAuth for authentication).
  4. **Integration Patterns**: The **component & connector perspective** of ADLs (e.g., MontiArc) suggests a **service-oriented or microservices-based integration pattern**, where DT modules expose **well-defined interfaces** for external consumption. The document’s emphasis on **stakeholder integration** further implies **hybrid integration patterns**, combining **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines** for batch data with **streaming architectures** (e.g., Apache Kafka) for real-time updates. No specific mention of **DT federation** or **inter-DT communication protocols** is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to integrate heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Key modeling languages and tools include:
  - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for structural and behavioral modeling, particularly **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector perspectives, and **state machines** (SysML v1.7) for behavioral descriptions.
  - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL (Architecture Analysis & Design Language), and MontiArc**, which define **components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition**, with extensions like **MontiArc Automaton** for behavioral modeling.
  - **Simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) and **general-purpose programming languages** (e.g., **C++, Java**) for detailed behavioral and analytical modeling.
  Data sources are aggregated from **physical systems** (via sensors/actuators) and processed through **digital shadow components**, which provide **contextual data traces** and their **abstraction** for specific DT purposes. Model relationships are established via **cross-tool integration**, ensuring consistency across **geometric (e.g., CAD models), behavioral (e.g., state machines), and analytical (e.g., simulation outputs)** representations. The DT constellation relies on **heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives** and **multi-notation modeling** to maintain coherence.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **fidelity and validity considerations** through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) validation and verification (V&V) processes**, though specific methods are not detailed in the provided document. Key aspects include:
  - **Model fidelity requirements** derived from **abstraction layers** (e.g., functional vs. technical) and **stakeholder-specific perspectives**, ensuring alignment with physical system behavior.
  - **Uncertainty sources** arise from **data abstraction** in digital shadows and **model-tool heterogeneity**, necessitating **quantification mechanisms** (e.g., statistical analysis of sensor data or simulation discrepancies).
  - **Error detection and correction** may involve **cross-model validation** (e.g., comparing SysML IBDs with Simulink simulations) and **automated consistency checks** across tools like **ACME or MontiArc**.
  - **Quality assurance** is implied through **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) but lacks explicit mention of **formal verification protocols** or **real-time error correction frameworks**. The document emphasizes the need for **active DT components** to process and interpret data, suggesting **runtime validation** as a critical but unspecified process.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The data flow from physical to digital in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized as an **automated one-way communication** between the state of an existing physical object and its digital representation, as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. This interaction involves **contextual data traces** collected from the physical system, which are then **aggregated and abstracted** for specific analytical or monitoring purposes. While the document does not specify exact **data types** (e.g., sensor readings, IoT telemetry, operational logs), the process implies reliance on **heterogeneous data sources** (e.g., sensors, PLCs, or embedded systems) transmitting data via **industrial communication protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, or proprietary protocols). The **transmission frequency** depends on the application but is typically **event-driven or time-triggered** (e.g., real-time updates for critical parameters or periodic polling for non-critical data). **Data quality assurance** is implied but not detailed; however, the integration of **modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink) suggests preprocessing steps like **data normalization, filtering, or validation** to ensure consistency before aggregation. **Data integration mechanisms** involve combining disparate data streams into a unified digital representation, often leveraging **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) to align with functional/logical architectures described via **ADLs** (e.g., SysML IBDs, MontiArc).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical network infrastructure for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the broader context of **MBSE and system modeling** implies reliance on **industrial-grade communication protocols** (e.g., OPC UA for interoperability, MQTT for lightweight IoT messaging) to facilitate data exchange between physical and digital layers. The **network topology** and **architecture** are likely **hybrid**, combining **edge computing nodes** (for low-latency local processing) and **centralized cloud/on-premise servers** (for storage and analytics). While **bandwidth and latency requirements** are not quantified, the document emphasizes the need for **real-time or near-real-time data flows** (e.g., for digital shadows). **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** may include **failover protocols, data replication, or fault-tolerant architectures**, though specifics are absent. **Security protocols** for network communication are implied but not specified; however, industrial DTs typically employ **encryption (TLS/SSL), authentication (OAuth, certificates), and access control** to protect data integrity and confidentiality.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The deployment infrastructure for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly described in the document. However, the **MBSE context** suggests support for **multi-tiered deployment models**, including **cloud-based** (for scalability and global access), **edge computing** (for low-latency processing near physical assets), and **on-premise** (for proprietary or high-security environments). **Computational and storage requirements** are inferred to be **highly variable**, depending on the system’s complexity (e.g., real-time simulations may demand **GPU-accelerated servers** or **distributed computing clusters**). **Scalability mechanisms** likely involve **elastic resource allocation** (e.g., Kubernetes, serverless architectures) and **modular microservices** to handle dynamic workloads. **Availability and reliability assurance** may rely on **redundant infrastructure, load balancing, and automated failover**, though no specific technologies (e.g., AWS, Azure, or private cloud solutions) are mentioned. The document does not specify whether **hybrid deployment models** (combining cloud/edge/on-premise) are supported, but the integration of **heterogeneous tools** (e.g., Simulink, SysML) implies flexibility in deployment strategies.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) engineering and development process leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, and **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition, with extensions like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines** for behavioral modeling. Behavioral details are further refined using tools like **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]), or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**). The process integrates heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and tools, requiring seamless interoperability between modeling notations and platforms. **Quality assurance** is implicitly tied to the rigor of MBSE methodologies, while **version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly detailed but are inferred as critical for maintaining consistency across iterative model refinements and system updates. The transition between phases (e.g., from logical to product architecture) is governed by the chosen methodology (e.g., RFLP’s phased approach) and toolchain integration.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates **safety-critical system considerations** through the **safety cell**, which restricts the maximum working range of the first vertical axis to **[-90°, 90°]** and enforces operational boundaries to prevent collisions or unsafe conditions. **Fail-safe mechanisms** are implicitly supported by the **dual encoder system** (motor encoder and secondary encoder behind the gearbox), which mitigates backlash and positional uncertainty by cross-referencing encoder readings to infer elasticity and friction in the drive train. **Cybersecurity protocols** are indirectly addressed via the **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl CNC controller**, a specialized system for subtractive manufacturing (e.g., milling) with **access control mechanisms** for axis-specific inputs (position, speed, acceleration) and outputs (target/actual values of position, derivatives, and motor current). The system’s **risk assessment** is embedded in its design, as the **gear transmission and cycloidal gear** ensure torque and speed constraints (e.g., **|q̇_max| = 75°/s**) are met, while the **permanent magnet synchronous motor**’s torque (**τ_M**) and current (**I_M**) are monitored for operational integrity. No explicit mention of **authentication systems**, **threat mitigation strategies**, or **data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR, ISO 27001) is provided in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
1) _Problem identification:_ To get all relevant requirements,
one has to describe the existing problem, the scope of the
considerations, the scope of the solution, and the purpose
why a DS is created.
2) _Asset and model analysis:_ One has to choose the parts of
the asset that are relevant for the defined DS purpose and
analyze from which data sources the data needed can be
covered. This leads to a description of the asset and the
data structure.

3) _Use or creation of calculations or models:_ As existing
data might not be sufficient to fulfil the purpose, we



can use existing or create new calculations, simulation
models, or data aggregation methods.
4) _Data identification:_ We have to identify relevant data
traces, data points, and their metadata.


III. U SE C ASE


The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX
100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic
AG (Fig. 2 top). Due to its flexible rotary joints, high path
repeatability (0 _._ 06 mm) and positional accuracy (0 _._ 1 mm), as
well as its functional reach of 2 m, it is suitable for complex
manufacturing tasks such as machining [44]. The overarching
research task of this industrial robot is machining. The first
vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital
shadow.

Fig. 2 bottom sketches the functional structure. The first
axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with
the current _I_ _M_ as input and torque _τ_ _M_ as output. To achieve
the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used
(with torque _τ_ _G_ as output), followed by a cycloidal gear (with
torque _τ_ _out_ as output). The safety cell of the robot restricts the
maximum working range to [ _−_ 90 _[◦]_ _,_ 90 _[◦]_ ], and the maximum
achievable speed is _|q_ ˙ max _|_ = 75 _[◦]_ _/_ s. There are two position
encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly
on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.
This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional
uncertainty, as the difference between these encoders allows
conclusions to be drawn about the elasticity and friction of the
entire drive train.

The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D
sl. This Computer Numerical Control (CNC) controller is
particularly suitable for subtractive manufacturing processes
such as milling. The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs. Possible
outputs are the target and actual values of the position and its
derivatives, as well as the motor current. The motor current
can be converted into the applied torque using known motor

constants.
